title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator

statement.markdown: Implement a generic math calculator that can perform addition and subtraction of two numbers. The calculator should handle both primitive types (number) and custom objects. However, the type inference for the calculator is incorrect and results in a type error when trying to subtract two custom objects.
statement.inputFormat: The function will be called with two arguments: the first is a number or a custom object with a numeric value, and the second is a number or a custom object with a numeric value.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return the result of the operation (either addition or subtraction).
statement.notes: The custom objects should have a numeric value property, and the calculator should handle both primitive types and custom objects correctly.

constraints.description: Input values are within this range
Input values are within this range

examples.explanation: Addition of two custom objects with numeric values
Addition of a number and a custom object with a numeric value
Subtraction of two custom objects with numeric values

hints.text: Check the type inference for the calculator function

editorial.title: Editorial title
editorial.intuition: The type inference for the calculator function is incorrect because it is trying to subtract two custom objects without checking if they have a numeric value property.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to modify the calculator function to check if the arguments are custom objects with a numeric value property before attempting to subtract them.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to modify the calculator function to use a generic type constraint that checks if the arguments are numbers or custom objects with a numeric value property.
commonMistakes: Trying to subtract custom objects without checking for numeric value property

functionSignature.javascript: function calculator(a: T, b: T): T
functionSignature.python: def calculate(self, a: typing.Any, b: typing.Any) -> typing.Any:
